privacy_data_protection.description: The extent to which digital euro transactions and user data will be private, balancing user anonymity with anti-money laundering (AML) and combating the financing of terrorism (CFT) requirements.
privacy_data_protection.salience: Highly contested by civil society organisations, privacy advocates, and some MEPs demanding robust safeguards, against financial institutions and law enforcement agencies stressing the need for traceability to combat illicit activities.

role_of_intermediaries_and_disintermedia.description: Defining the roles and responsibilities of commercial banks and other payment service providers in distributing the digital euro, and mitigating risks of financial disintermediation from the traditional banking sector.
role_of_intermediaries_and_disintermedia.salience: Commercial banks lobby for their continued central role and against measures allowing the ECB to directly disintermediate them or leading to large-scale deposit outflows. Fintechs might lobby for a more open distribution model.

legal_tender_status_and_mandatory_accept.description: Whether the digital euro will be granted full legal tender status and if its acceptance by merchants and public bodies will be mandatory across the Eurozone, ensuring its wide usability.
legal_tender_status_and_mandatory_accept.salience: Merchant associations often oppose mandatory acceptance due to potential costs of new infrastructure and payment processing fees. Consumer groups and the ECB generally support it to ensure wide usability and accessibility as a public good.

offline_functionality_and_accessibility.description: The requirement and technical feasibility of providing robust offline payment capabilities for the digital euro, ensuring accessibility for all citizens, including vulnerable groups and those lacking internet access.
offline_functionality_and_accessibility.salience: Consumer organisations and social rights groups strongly advocate for robust offline functionality. Technical implementers (banks, tech companies) might raise concerns about security, cost, and complexity of implementation.

limits_on_holdings_and_remuneration.description: The proposed limits on the amount of digital euro individuals can hold and whether these holdings will be remunerated, impacting financial stability and the transmission of monetary policy.
limits_on_holdings_and_remuneration.salience: Commercial banks lobby for strict limits and no remuneration to prevent large-scale deposit flight from their accounts. The ECB seeks flexibility for monetary policy, while consumer groups might advocate for higher limits or potential remuneration.

cost_recovery_and_funding_model.description: Determining how the costs for developing, operating, and maintaining the digital euro infrastructure and services will be recovered and funded across the ecosystem.
cost_recovery_and_funding_model.salience: Commercial banks and payment service providers will want to avoid bearing the full burden of infrastructure and service costs without appropriate compensation or revenue streams. Member States and the ECB will debate public funding vs. user or intermediary fees.

innovation_and_competition_in_payments.description: The impact of the digital euro on private sector innovation and competition within the European payments market, particularly regarding new payment solutions and business models.
innovation_and_competition_in_payments.salience: Fintechs and some technology companies might argue the digital euro could stifle private innovation if it's too dominant or restrictive. Established payment providers might see it as a threat or an opportunity depending on their integration strategy. Policymakers aim for a balance.

governance_and_oversight.description: The framework for the governance, supervision, and democratic accountability regarding the design, operation, and evolution of the digital euro by EU institutions and Member States.
governance_and_oversight.salience: Member States and the European Parliament will likely seek to ensure appropriate democratic oversight and accountability of the ECB's role, balancing its independence with public scrutiny over a critical public infrastructure.

interoperability_and_cross_border_paymen.description: Ensuring the digital euro's interoperability with existing national and international payment systems and its potential use for efficient cross-border transactions and international cooperation.
interoperability_and_cross_border_paymen.salience: Financial institutions and businesses involved in cross-border trade will lobby for seamless integration and international standards. The ECB might seek to enhance the euro's international role through robust cross-border functionality, while other countries might have concerns about financial sovereignty.

privacy_data_protection.description: The extent to which digital euro transactions and user data will be private, balancing user anonymity with anti-money laundering (AML) and combating the financing of terrorism (CFT) requirements.
